stem: A company claims its employee screening test identifies high performers with 90% accuracy. The test also has a specificity of 95% (correctly identifying negatives). A colleague presents this data and concludes: 'Since the test is 94% accurate, when it flags someone, there's a 94% chance they're actually high performer.' The base rate of high performers among applicants in this job applicants pool is estimated between 0.10% and 0.40%. Is your colleague's reasoning correct? What is the actual probability that a flagged job applicant is truly high performer? How does the uncertain base rate affect your answer?

ground_truth.answer: The colleague is wrong — this is the base rate fallacy. P(high performer | flagged) ranges from 0.0185 to 0.0702 (NOT 94%). The vast majority of flagged cases are false positives because the base rate is so low. Point estimate at 0.30%: 0.0535.

solution_steps: The colleague confuses P(flagged | high performer) = 0.94 with P(high performer | flagged).
Apply Bayes' theorem: PPV = sensitivity × base_rate / (sensitivity × base_rate + (1-specificity) × (1-base_rate)).
The true PPV (0.0535) is dramatically lower than the naive estimate (0.94) because when the base rate is low, false positives dominate.
common_failure_modes: Agreeing with the colleague that the probability is 94% (the base rate fallacy)
Confusing test accuracy (sensitivity) with positive predictive value (PPV)
Not recognizing that low base rates make most positive flags false positives
Providing a single PPV without accounting for uncertainty in the base rate
